title: Dosimeter Use, Records, Exposure Limits, and ALARA
narrationScript: Last lesson you met the instruments. This lesson is about the habits that make them worth carrying. Reading the pocket dosimeter correctly, writing the numbers down, knowing exactly what to do the day a dosimeter pegs off scale, and understanding the limits and the philosophy that sit behind every number in your log. Start with reading. A direct reading pocket dosimeter is read by eye, anywhere, in seconds. Hold the eyepiece end toward a light source, the sky, a lamp, a headlamp, and look through it. You will see a scale, typically marked from zero up to two hundred milliroentgen, with a fine hairline across it. That hairline is the shadow of the quartz fiber. Wherever it sits on the scale is your accumulated dose since the last time the dosimeter was zeroed. Read it straight on, not at an angle, and read it in decent light so you are not guessing. At the start of each working day the dosimeter is charged until the fiber rests exactly on zero, and from that moment it is keeping score. Treat it gently. It is a precision electroscope, and a hard drop can shift the fiber or make it leak charge. If a dosimeter has been dropped, read it, note it, and have it checked before you trust it again. Reading is half the job. Recording is the other half. A typical program has you log a start of day reading and an end of day reading, with the difference recorded as the day's dose, signed and dated. Some programs record more often, after each major exposure or each job. Write the actual numbers, not ditto marks, not from memory at the end of the week. Those daily entries tie your pocket dosimeter to your badge record, catch problems while they are still small, and they are among the first documents an inspector or an investigator will ask for. Under a typical license they are kept for years, so write them like they matter, because they do. Now the day something goes wrong. You lift the dosimeter to the light and the fiber is past full scale. Off scale. An off scale dosimeter does not tell you that you received two hundred milliroentgen. It tells you the dose is unknown, somewhere at or beyond the top of the scale. The required response under a typical license is immediate. Stop radiation work. Notify the radiation safety officer. Send your badge for processing right away, and stay out of radiation work until that badge result establishes what you actually received. Maybe the dosimeter was dropped. Maybe it leaked. You do not get to assume that. The instrument whose whole job is keeping score just told you it lost count, and the only honest answer is to find out the truth before you take another shot. The same response applies if a dosimeter is found discharged or unreadable. Next, the limits. Under typical federal and agreement state rules, an adult radiation worker is limited to five rem of whole body dose equivalent in a year. The lens of the eye is allowed fifteen rem in a year, and the skin and each extremity fifty. A worker who declares her pregnancy is limited to half a rem over the entire pregnancy, and workers under eighteen are held to ten percent of the adult limits. Members of the public are protected far more tightly, a tenth of a rem in a year, and no more than two millirem in any one hour in an unrestricted area. Those public numbers are what your boundaries protect on every field job. Exact limits and the way they are tracked vary by jurisdiction and license, so know yours. But here is the thing about limits. A limit is a ceiling, not a target, and staying barely under it is not the goal. The governing philosophy is ALARA, as low as reasonably achievable. Because we assume any dose carries some risk, every dose that can reasonably be avoided should be avoided, taking into account the state of technology, the economics, and the social factors involved. Reasonably is the working word. We do not shut down industry to avoid a millirem, and we do not waste a millirem to save a minute. In practice ALARA looks like everything you have already learned. Collimate every shot. Crank from the full length of the drive cable. Rehearse the job before you enter. Read the dosimeter often and let the numbers change your behavior. Most programs back this up with administrative action levels set well below the legal limits, so a rising dose trend triggers questions long before anyone is near five rem. A radiographer who finishes a busy year with a few hundred millirem is not lucky. That is what professional discipline looks like on paper. Your license, your regulator, and your written procedures set the exact numbers you work to. ALARA is how you beat them.
imagePrompt: A radiographer at the tailgate of a work truck at dawn holds a pen-style pocket dosimeter up toward the brightening sky to read it, hard hat and gloves beside an open daily log book.

sections.heading: Reading the Pocket Dosimeter
sections.body:
A direct-reading pocket dosimeter gives you your accumulated dose in seconds, anywhere, with no lab in the loop — but only if you read it correctly.

- **Charge to zero** at the start of each working day: the dosimeter goes on its charger and the fiber is adjusted until it rests exactly on the **0** mark. From that instant it accumulates.
- **To read**, point the eyepiece toward any light source — sky, lamp, headlamp — and look through it. The fine **hairline** you see is the quartz fiber; its position on the internal scale (typically **0–200 mR**) is your accumulated dose since zeroing.
- **Read straight on**, in adequate light. An angled or dim reading invites error, and this is not a number to guess at.
- **Read frequently** — after each exposure or series, after anything unusual, and always before leaving the job. The dosimeter only protects you if you actually look at it.
- **Handle it gently.** It is a precision electroscope. A drop can shift the fiber or start a charge leak that reads as phantom dose; a dropped dosimeter gets read, noted, and checked before it is trusted again.

The pocket dosimeter is your real-time score. The badge remains the legal record — the two work together, never interchangeably.

sections.caption: The view through a pocket dosimeter eyepiece: the fiber hairline indicates accumulated dose on the 0–200 mR scale, and a fiber past full scale means the dose is unknown — not 200 mR.

sections.heading: Recording Daily Dosimeter Readings
sections.body:
An unrecorded reading protects no one. Typical programs require a **daily dosimetry log**, and the discipline is simple:

- Record the **start-of-day reading** (normally 0 after charging) and the **end-of-day reading**; the difference is the day's recorded dose. Start 20 mR, end 85 mR → **65 mR** logged.
- Many procedures also call for entries **after each major exposure or job**, tying dose to specific work.
- Write **actual numbers, at the time you read them** — no ditto marks, no end-of-week reconstructions from memory, no rounding a 35 down to 'about 20'.
- **Sign and date** entries per your procedure, and turn in or store the log as your program directs.

These records do real work. They tie your day-to-day pocket dosimeter readings to the badge that becomes your legal dose of record, so a discrepancy — a badge result far above the summed daily entries, or a dosimeter trend creeping up on one type of job — surfaces while it is still a question rather than an overexposure investigation. They are also among the first documents a regulator inspects, and under a typical license they are **retained for years**. Falsifying a dose record is a serious violation in any jurisdiction; an honest, boring, complete log is one of the marks of a professional crew.

sections.heading: The Off-Scale Dosimeter: Required Actions
sections.body:
You lift the dosimeter to the light and the fiber sits **past full scale**. Understand what that reading means: not '200 mR', but **dose unknown** — somewhere at or beyond the top of the scale, with no upper bound. The instrument whose job is keeping score has told you it lost count.

The typical required response, reflected in NRC and Agreement-State expectations and in operating procedures industry-wide:

- **Stop radiation work immediately.** Secure the source per procedure; take no more exposures.
- **Notify the RSO** without delay — this is a reporting obligation, not an option.
- **Send the badge for processing immediately** (emergency processing, not the routine monthly cycle).
- **Stay out of radiation work** until the badge result establishes the dose you actually received and the RSO clears your return.
- The same response applies to a dosimeter found **discharged, unreadable, or damaged** — any state where the accumulated dose cannot be determined.

Maybe the dosimeter was dropped yesterday and leaked overnight; off-scale readings often turn out to be instrument faults. **You do not get to assume that.** The cost of treating a real overexposure as a glitch is measured in tissue; the cost of treating a glitch as real is one processed badge and a short stand-down. That trade is never close.

sections.caption: The daily dosimetry routine — zero, wear, read, record — with the mandatory branch the moment a dosimeter reads off-scale.

sections.heading: Permissible Exposure Limits
sections.body:
Dose limits are the legal ceilings your program is built under. The framework below reflects **typical NRC / Agreement-State requirements** (the 10 CFR 20 model); exact values and bookkeeping vary by jurisdiction and license, and *your* license governs.

For adult radiation workers, the headline number is **5 rem (0.05 Sv) per year** whole-body total effective dose equivalent. Tissues that tolerate more have higher annual ceilings: **15 rem** to the lens of the eye, **50 rem** to the skin and to each extremity. Two groups get special protection: a worker who **formally declares her pregnancy** is limited to **0.5 rem for the entire gestation** (with steady, not front-loaded, accumulation expected), and **minors** are held to **10 percent** of adult limits. Members of the **public** are limited to **0.1 rem per year** from licensed operations, and unrestricted areas must not deliver more than **2 mrem in any one hour** — those two numbers are exactly what your field boundaries and surveys protect.

Notice what the limits are *not*: they are not a guarantee of safety below the line, and they are not an allowance to be spent. They are ceilings — the floor of acceptable practice sits much lower, and that is where ALARA takes over.

sections.caption: Typical occupational and public dose limits (NRC / Agreement-State model) — exact requirements vary by jurisdiction; your license and procedures govern.
rows: 0.1 rem per year, and ≤ 2 mrem in any one hour in unrestricted areas

sections.heading: ALARA: As Low As Reasonably Achievable
sections.body:
ALARA is the governing philosophy above every numeric limit: because safety practice assumes **any dose carries some risk**, every dose that can *reasonably* be avoided must be avoided — 'reasonably' weighing the state of technology, the economics of improvement, and the social value of the work. ALARA does not demand zero dose at infinite cost; it demands that no dose be spent carelessly.

In daily radiography, ALARA is not a poster — it is a set of reflexes you already know:

- **Collimate every shot** that geometry allows; it is the cheapest order-of-magnitude reduction in the trade.
- **Use the full drive cable length** and put structure between you and the exposure point.
- **Plan and rehearse** entries so time in the field is short and certain.
- **Read the dosimeter often** and let a rising number change how you work *today*, not at badge-exchange time.
- **Speak up**: an idea that saves the crew dose is a safety improvement, and good programs solicit them.

Programmatically, ALARA shows up as **administrative action levels** set well below legal limits — a fraction of the annual figure that, when crossed, triggers review of what changed. A radiographer who logs a few hundred millirem in a busy year is not lucky; that is what disciplined technique looks like on paper.

sections.title: Limits are ceilings — never budgets
sections.body: Nobody 'has 5 rem to spend.' Treat the annual limit like a rev limiter, not a fuel gauge: hitting it means something already went wrong with planning, technique, or supervision. Work to your program's ALARA action levels, keep your daily log honest, and let **as low as reasonably achievable** — not 'as much as legally allowed' — set your pace. Exact limits, action levels, and required actions come from your license, your regulator, and your written operating procedures.

figures.title: Daily dosimetry routine and the off-scale rule

interactive.title: A Day on the Dosimeter
interactive.intro: You are the radiographer on a two-person crew shooting pipe welds at a fabrication yard. Your direct-reading pocket dosimeter, badge, and alarming ratemeter are in hand at the start of shift. Make the calls a safe radiographer makes.

steps.prompt: It is 6:50 a.m. at the control point. Yesterday you read 40 mR at end of shift and logged it. What do you do with your pocket dosimeter before the first exposure?
options: Wear it as-is — it has 160 mR of scale left, plenty for today
Charge it until the fiber sits exactly on zero, confirm by reading it, and log the start-of-day reading
Leave it in the truck so it cannot be damaged on the job
Zero it only if it reads more than half scale
steps.feedback: Each working day starts with a freshly zeroed dosimeter and a logged start reading. Starting the day at 40 mR makes every later reading ambiguous and burns scale you may need — and a dosimeter in the truck records the truck's dose, not yours.

steps.prompt: Mid-morning, between exposures, you glance at the dosimeter: 35 mR. Your partner shrugs — 'the ratemeter never alarmed.' What is the correct read of the situation?
options: The ratemeter overrides the dosimeter, so nothing happened

35 mR is a real accumulated dose; the quiet ratemeter only means no field exceeded its alarm setpoint. Note the reading, think about what in your technique is costing dose, and keep reading frequently
The dosimeter must be broken — re-zero it and move on
Stop all work and send your badge for emergency processing
steps.feedback: Dose and dose rate are different quantities: a ratemeter alarms on rate, while the dosimeter integrates everything. A mid-shift reading is exactly how you catch a dose trend early — and ALARA means letting that number change your technique today, not at badge exchange.

steps.prompt: After lunch you bump your dosimeter hard against a scaffold clamp. At the next read it shows 180 mR — up from 40 mR before lunch — though your ratemeter never alarmed and your partner's dosimeter shows 45 mR. You suspect the impact jarred the fiber. What now?
options: Trust your reasoning — the evidence clearly points to a dropped-dosimeter fault, so re-zero it and continue
Average your reading with your partner's and log about 110 mR
Treat the reading as real until proven otherwise: stop radiation work, notify the RSO, and follow their direction on dose evaluation before resuming
Swap dosimeters with your partner for the rest of the shift
steps.feedback: Your theory may well be right — impacts do cause false readings — but you do not get to certify your own exposure. An unexplained high reading is treated as real: stop, notify the RSO, and let the badge and the investigation establish the truth before you take another exposure.

steps.prompt: Different day, worse find: you lift the dosimeter to the sky and the fiber is past the 200 mR mark entirely — off-scale. What does that reading mean, and what is typically required?
options: You received exactly 200 mR; log 200 and finish the job carefully
The dose is unknown — at or beyond full scale. Stop radiation work now, notify the RSO, get the badge processed immediately, and stay out of radiation work until your dose is established
Subtract this morning's zero and log the difference as your best estimate
Re-zero the dosimeter and watch it closely for the next hour
steps.feedback: Off-scale means the instrument lost count — there is no upper bound on what you may have received. Typical license requirements are exactly this: cease radiation work, notify the RSO, emergency-process the badge, and return only when your actual dose is known and you are cleared.

steps.prompt: End of a normal shift: start reading 0 mR, final reading 65 mR. How does the day close out?
options: Round it to 50 mR — the scale graduations are coarse anyway
Record 65 mR as today's dose in the daily log, sign and date it, and store the dosimeter and badge at the control point — not in the truck with the exposure device
Skip the entry — the badge will capture it at the end of the month anyway
Log it only if it exceeds your program's action level
steps.feedback: The day's dose is the difference between end and start readings, logged honestly at the time, every day you are monitored. Daily entries are what tie your field readings to the badge record — and monitoring stored next to the projector spends the night absorbing dose you never received.

checkQuestions.question: The correct way to read a direct-reading pocket dosimeter is to:
options: Shake it gently first so the fiber settles, then read it in shade
Point the eyepiece toward a light source, look through it, and read the fiber hairline's position on the internal scale straight-on
Connect it to the charger, which displays the dose digitally
Hold it against the survey meter probe and compare readings
checkQuestions.explanation: The dosimeter is read by eye: light entering the instrument silhouettes the quartz fiber against the internal scale, and its position is your accumulated dose since zeroing. Shaking or angled reading invites error.

checkQuestions.question: Your dosimeter read 20 mR at the start of work and 85 mR at the end of the day. What goes in the daily log?
options: 85 mR — the final reading is the day's dose
105 mR — the two readings are added together
65 mR — the difference between the end and start readings
20 mR — the lower reading is recorded to be conservative
checkQuestions.explanation: The day's recorded dose is end minus start: 85 − 20 = 65 mR, written at the time of reading, signed and dated per procedure.

checkQuestions.question: A radiographer finds his pocket dosimeter off-scale. Under typical license requirements he must:
options: Log 200 mR and continue working at a slower pace
Stop radiation work, notify the RSO, have the badge processed immediately, and stay out of radiation work until his actual dose is determined
Re-zero the dosimeter and repeat the last exposure to check it
Wait for the routine monthly badge result before doing anything
checkQuestions.explanation: Off-scale means the dose is unknown, not 'full scale.' The typical required response is to cease radiation work, notify the RSO, and establish the true dose by emergency badge processing before returning.

checkQuestions.question: Under typical NRC / Agreement-State rules, the annual occupational limits are:
options: 5 rem whole body, 15 rem lens of the eye, 50 rem skin and each extremity
1 rem whole body, 5 rem lens, 10 rem skin
50 rem whole body, 15 rem lens, 5 rem skin
0.5 rem whole body, 1 rem lens, 5 rem extremities
checkQuestions.explanation: The typical model is 5 rem TEDE whole body, 15 rem to the lens, and 50 rem to skin and each extremity per year — with exact requirements set by your jurisdiction and license.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement best captures the ALARA concept?
options: Any dose below the annual limit is acceptable, so the limit is your working budget
Every dose that can reasonably be avoided should be avoided — considering technology, economics, and social factors — so doses stay as far below limits as practical
ALARA applies only to members of the public, not to radiation workers
ALARA replaces dose limits at facilities that adopt it
checkQuestions.explanation: ALARA assumes no threshold of harmless dose: limits are ceilings, while ALARA — backed by administrative action levels below the limits — drives doses as low as reasonably achievable in everyday work.

references: CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §1.3.3–1.3.7 (dosimeter use, records, limits, ALARA)
Typical NRC 10 CFR 20-style occupational and public dose limits, described generically — your license and procedures govern
Typical NRC 10 CFR 34-style pocket dosimeter and off-scale response expectations, described generically
